description: A surface or material is 'wirua' when liquid lies on it or has soaked in. The word also covers wet weather and air laden with moisture. Water is the usual liquid, but spilled oil can make cloth wet as well; context and degree words carry the amount.
articulatory_notes: Rounded lips and a raised back tongue form 'w' before high-front 'i'. A tap or short trill begins the stressed second syllable and releases into rounded high-back 'u'. Final open central 'a' follows in hiatus as a separate syllable.
usage_notes: Wet earth is 'wirua muila', and wet cloth is 'wirua wetha'. The form 'wirua howenu' describes wet weather. The ecological expression 'wirua muila menuro' describes a wet-land habitat without assigning a scientific or legal wetland category.

semantic_domains.nature: moisture present in land, weather, air, or living surfaces
semantic_domains.physical: liquid coating a surface or absorbed into a material
